schools.summary: Samuel retired to Ramah; God blames not the mourning but its excess; the horn of oil, unlike Saul's brittle vial, speaks of plentiful, durable grace.
The sentence on Saul is irreversible, so Samuel's affectionate grief is now in vain.
The rejection's consequences unfold at once; the book's pathos over Saul is marked, and Bethlehem gains its strange notoriety as David's (and Christ's) cradle.
'Mourn' implies Samuel prayed for Saul's restoration; the oil is designation, not inauguration; 'I have provided me a king' is emphatic against the people's self-willed choice.
Gill finds a weakness of faith and fear of man, since the God who sent Samuel could protect him.
The fear was well grounded: Saul was already given to the evil spirit, and the Lord, not blaming Samuel, showed him a safe way.
Clarke defends the 'sacrifice' as strictly true: one need not tell the whole truth, but must tell nothing but the truth.
Henry allows both that the best men's faith is imperfect and that Samuel sought prudent direction from heaven.

debates.question: Was Samuel's fear of Saul a fault or well founded?

sides.position: a weakness of faith and fear of man
well grounded, since the Lord did not blame it but provided a way

schools.summary: The offerer may invite family and friends to feast on the peace offering, so Jesse's house is called.
After the blood and fat were offered, the worshippers feasted on the flesh of the sacrifice.
Anointing marked priest, prophet, and above all king ('the Lord's anointed'); David is anointed thrice, all as types of the Messiah, the Anointed One.
The elders, knowing Samuel came unexpectedly to reprove wrongdoing, tremble lest his sudden visit to obscure Bethlehem bring judgment.
They feared the strangeness of the visit; 'comest thou in peace' simply means 'is all well?'
Guilt causes fear; it becomes us to tremble at God's word, as all Jerusalem was troubled at the birth of the King.
Since the ark left the tabernacle, Samuel sacrificed in various places; 'sanctify yourselves' means the washings that preceded a sacrificial feast.
Sanctifying is washing and preparing the mind by prayer; Wesley notes the singular care shown to Jesse and his sons, invited to the feast itself.
Samuel's word was true, sacrifice being one real end though not the only one.
Before solemn ordinances a solemn preparation; Christ too came peaceably to sacrifice, bringing his own offering.
Eliab's tall, handsome form recalls Saul; the Lord rejects him, looking not at the outward figure but the heart.
Even a prophet, speaking his own thought, may err as Nathan did; God corrects Samuel by a secret whisper, for men judge by the eyes, God does not.
Wesley: Samuel was mistaken, as prophets were when they hastily spoke their own thoughts before consulting God.
Eyes against heart is outward form against inward reality; on that ground the Lord rejected Eliab.
Clarke: man may look on the outward, but to sound the heart is to usurp God's prerogative; the revelation shows a holy familiarity with God.
God both knows the heart and judges by it; a meek and quiet spirit is of great price, and true beauty is within.
Abinadab passes and is refused; Samuel had made his errand known to Jesse, and God alone decides among the gallant sons.
God magnifies his sovereignty, passing by the most promising; the elder shall serve the younger.
Shammah, the third son, is likewise passed over; the accumulation of refusals throws God's silence into relief.
God's choices cannot be predicted from outward advantage or family rank; the field is steadily narrowed to make the choice plainly his.
The seven include four more sons; the genealogy names only seven of Jesse's eight, one having apparently died young.
The review was private, before the public feast; one son unnamed either died young or was born of a concubine.
Gill counts the sons carefully, naming Nathanael, Raddai, and Ozem, the fourth nowhere recorded.
Samuel is momentarily uncertain; the overlooked youngest, small and ruddy, is kept with the sheep, held of little account by his father.
Jesse judged the shepherd boy most unfit; God so ordered it that David's election appear his own work; 'David' means beloved, type of the Beloved Son.
God exalts those men despise; David is taken from the ewes as Moses from the flock, the pastoral life a school for kingship.
'Ruddy' is the reddish hair prized in the south; beauty was a fitting outward distinction of a king, and the Lord points David out.
No notice is taken of his mean shepherd's dress; his sweet, unstately look betokened an amiable temper.

themes: comeliness not the ground of choice

schools.summary: No explanatory word is recorded; Samuel likely kept the high destiny secret, adopting David as pupil; the Spirit given is the Holy Ghost, opposed to evil spirits.
The Spirit conferred wisdom, prudence, courage, and magnanimity; the brethren saw the unction but knew not it was to the kingdom.
The anointing designates David to the government and communicates fitting graces, making him a type of the Anointed One; the seal of the Spirit is the best evidence of election.
The departed Spirit was that of prophecy, wisdom for rule, and courage, all now withdrawn from Saul.
Forsaking God, Saul is forsaken by grace; where the good Spirit departs the evil takes possession, and he becomes a terror to himself.
Keil and Delitzsch: not mere melancholy but a higher evil power possessing Saul and driving him at times to madness.
The malady is mixed, natural and diabolical; 'from the Lord' means by permission, Saul delivered up to be buffeted of Satan.

debates.question: What was the nature of Saul's 'evil spirit from the Lord'?

sides.position: a real evil power possessing him, working through body and passions
a mixed madness, natural melancholy exploited by Satan, sent by God's permission

schools.summary: The court diagnoses an incurable melancholy blazing into jealous rage and counsels music, whose power over the mind the ancients well knew.
Gill takes the speakers for courtiers or physicians observing the king's frantic, melancholy state.
Henry credits their honesty but wishes they had counselled repentance and sent for Samuel to pray.
The ancients' testimony to music's power is rehearsed (Pythagoras, Aesculapius); a skilled harper is sought for the king.
Wesley: music cannot touch the demon directly, but by sweetening the passions and humours it disposes Saul to resist him, as it prepared the prophets for the good Spirit.
Henry warns that mirth may drown the soul's care, yet grants music a lawful help had they joined a prophet's counsel, and is glad they sought no diviner.

debates.question: Can music affect the evil spirit itself?

sides.position: not the spirit directly, only the passions and humours it works through

schools.summary: Gill notes only that Saul approved and was pleased with his servants' advice.
Saul commands a skilled player sought, which opens the door for the young man to name the son of Jesse.
Providence concurs with grace to prepare God's servant: Saul unknowingly summons his own anointed successor.
The speaker is a young fellow-pupil, not Doeg; 'man of war' is explained by David's fights with lion and bear and Philistine raids on Bethlehem, before Goliath.
Gill rejects the legend that the envious Doeg spoke; it was rather a friend, guided by providence.
David's sudden repute is the effect of the Spirit received at his anointing; 'the Lord is with him' means God prospers all he does.
The hidden anointing could not be concealed; the Spirit made David shine in obscurity till his fame reached the court.

debates.question: Who was the servant that commended David, and how was he already 'a man of war'?

sides.position: an envious Doeg exposing David to danger
a friendly young fellow-pupil; his valour proven against lion, bear, and Philistine raiders

schools.summary: Saul sends for David rather than seizing him, though kings were warned to take men's sons.
Saul asks Jesse for his son, a request soon met with a present, in keeping with Israel's simple customs.
David, still 'with the sheep,' is brought to court without contrivance of his own, providence concurring with grace.
The homely present shows Israel's primitive customs; in the East subjection is owned with a gift, Jesse the sheik acknowledging his king.
Jesse sent his son and present readily, following providence cheerfully rather than forcing it.
Clarke advances Warburton's theory that these events are told out of order, after Goliath; Wesley notes the present suited the times and Jesse's mean estate.

debates.question: Do the events of vv.14-23 stand in chronological order?

sides.position: yes, as the text places them
no; an anticipation, these events following the combat with Goliath (Warburton)

schools.summary: Saul loves David and makes him armour-bearer, receiving his own benefactor and successor without suspicion; the later 'whose son is this?' is met by David's return home and Saul's failing memory.
David stood before Saul as a ministering servant.
Wesley: that Saul loved him shows something good remained in him; the office was conferred though David soon returned to his father.
Gill marks Saul's condescension in asking Jesse's leave rather than detaining David by force.
'Let David stand before me' means let him remain in service; David is fixed at court by the king's own desire, having found favour.
Gill relays Abarbanel's reading that the spirit was not evil but God's Spirit convicting Saul of his rejection.
Clarke weighs the text on 'evil' and calls the malady mixed; Wesley: the spirit departed only for a season, by a success partly natural and partly supernatural.
David's music was Saul's physic, making his spirit sedate; like closing shutters against the sun, it shut the passages by which the devil gained access.
Ellicott gathers Luther, Basil, and Maurice: music repels demons and points to a Deliverer who takes the vulture from the heart.

debates.question: Was the spirit truly evil, and did the music cure it?

sides.position: the word 'evil' is absent from the Hebrew; it may be God's convicting Spirit
a real (mixed) evil spirit, soothed only indirectly as the music calmed the humours it worked through

outliers.note: Gathers Luther, Basil, Browning, and Maurice, reading David's harp as a shadow of the great Deliverer who unbinds the sufferer.

themes: David a type of the Deliverer
